1.1: 1 Chronicles begins with nine chapters of genealogies (chs. 1-9) — from Adam to the post-exilic community. The genealogical prologue serves a theological purpose: to demonstrate the continuity of YHWH's covenant people through the Babylonian exile. The lists trace: Adam to Israel (ch. 1), the twelve tribes (chs. 2-9), with special focus on the line of David (ch. 3, which includes the post-exilic Davidic line down to the 6th generation after Zerubbabel — into the 5th century BCE). Matthew's genealogy (Matt 1:1-17) is a direct descendant of the Chronicler's method: beginning with Abraham, structured in three sets of fourteen, it traces the covenant line to the Messiah through the same Davidic focus that Chronicles establishes.
1.2: The three names — Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared (qênān mah˘l˘lʾêl yereḏ) — continue the asyndetic list: names only, no ‘begat’ verb. In Gen 5:9-20 each patriarch has a full entry with lifespan and the wayyôled formula. The Chronicler strips all that away — maximum genealogical compression. What Genesis takes three verses per name, Chronicles covers in one verse. The purpose is lineage, not biography: the claim that this thread of human beings leads unbroken to Israel and David.
1.3: Enoch, Methuselah, Lamech (ĥnôḵ mêtûšelaḥ lāmeḵ) — the three pre-Flood names before Noah. Enoch is theologically significant in Gen 5:24 (‘God took him’) and Heb 11:5 (translated by faith without seeing death), but the Chronicler omits this. The genealogy is purely structural: names as markers of the chain toward David, not occasions for biographical comment.
1.4: nôaḥ stands alone; the sentence then shifts: ‘The sons of Noah: Shem, Ham, and Japheth’ (bênê-nôaḥ šêm ĥām wāyepet). The syntactic break from name-only list to the explicit sons-of formula marks the major division point. At Noah the genealogy branches; what follows in vv5-23 is the Table of Nations (Gen 10 compressed). The break also signals theological weight: the flood divides primordial history from the nations, and the Shem line will carry the covenant forward.
1.5: The seven sons of Japheth (bênê yepet): Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, Tiras. Identical to Gen 10:2. The Japheth branch is listed first (eldest son), though the covenant passes through Shem. Ezek 38–39 uses Gog, Magog, Tubal, and Meshech as the great northern adversary — the Chronicler’s naming them grounds them within YHWH’s created order, not outside it.
1.6: Sons of Gomer (bênê gômer): Ashkenaz, Riphath, Togarmah. In Jer 51:27 Ashkenaz is summoned to fight against Babylon. The names of Gen 10 are not merely ancient ethnic labels but geo-political realities in the Chronicler’s world — he writes knowing that these table-of-nations names map onto known peoples and territories.
1.7: Sons of Javan (bênê yāwān): Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, Dodanim. Javan = Ionia (Greece/Aegean). Kittim = Cyprus or the western Mediterranean more broadly; in Dan 11:30 ‘ships of Kittim’ refers to Rome. Tarshish is the far western sea-route (Jonah 1:3; Ps 48:7). The Japheth branch charts the northern and western peoples.
1.8: Sons of Ham (bênê ĥām): Cush, Egypt (miṣrayim), Put, Canaan. miṣrayim is the standard Hebrew dual-form name for Egypt, referring to Upper and Lower Egypt. The Ham branch is theologically loaded for Israel: Canaan is the land Israel occupied; Egypt is the land of slavery; Cush is the region to Egypt’s south. These names frame the nations Israel will encounter as part of the single human family descended from Noah.
1.9: Sons of Cush (bênê kûš): Seba, Havilah, Sabta, Raamah, Sabteca; sons of Raamah: Sheba and Dedan. The Cush branch extends into southern Arabia and East Africa. Sheba appears in 2 Chr 9 (the queen of Sheba visits Solomon) — one of the distant nations that comes to honor the Davidic king. The genealogical root of Sheba in Cush’s line traces her people back to Noah.
1.10: wayyôleḏ kûš ʾet-nimrôḏ hûʾ hêḥêl lihyôt gibbôr bāʾāreṣ — ‘Cush fathered Nimrod; he was the first to be a mighty man on earth.’ The verbal form hêḥêl (began, Hiphil of ĥālal) + infinitive construct lihyôt signals inception: Nimrod was the initiator of a type. gibbôr (mighty one, champion) is the same term used for the primordial Nephilim (gibbôrîm) in Gen 6:4 — figures of ambiguous human greatness before the flood. Chronicles drops Gen 10:9’s qualifier ‘mighty hunter before YHWH’ and the proverb ‘like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before YHWH’ — focusing purely on his status as the first gibbôr. The city-building tradition (Gen 10:10-12, founding Babylon and Nineveh) is also omitted; the Chronicler streamlines to lineage.
1.11: miṣrayim fathered the Ludites, Anamites, Lehabites, Naphtuhites. These are gentilics — collective nouns for peoples derived from eponymous ancestors. The Hebrew form ending in -îm (plural) is the marker: lûḏîm, ʿănāmîm, etc. The ancestors are individuals; the descendants are peoples. The Hebrew reader understands that these names refer to nations, not persons.
1.12: Pathrusites (patrûsîm, from Pathros = Upper Egypt), Casluhites (kaslûḥîm) — ‘from whom the Philistines came’ (ʾăšer yāṣĕʾû miššāmm pêeštîm), and Caphtorites. The parenthetical identification of the Philistines’ origin inserts a politically charged note: the Philistines, Israel’s persistent enemy in the Davidic narrative (Chronicles focuses on David’s Philistine victories), are placed within the same genealogical structure as Israel. Amos 9:7: ‘Did I not bring the Philistines from Caphtor?’ — all ultimately under YHWH’s governance.
1.13: Canaan fathered Sidon (ṣîḏôn bêḵôrô, ‘his firstborn’) and Heth. bêḵôr (firstborn) is the only birth-order designation in the Ham genealogy — Sidon’s primacy in the Canaanite branch is noted. Heth is the ancestor of the Hittites; Abraham buys the cave of Machpelah from Ephron the Hittite (Gen 23), grounding the patriarchal land-purchase within this genealogical lineage.
1.14: The Jebusites (yêḇûsî), Amorites (ʾĕmôrî), Girgashites (girgāšî) — three of the seven Canaanite nations of Deut 7:1. The Jebusites are specifically the inhabitants of Jerusalem before David conquered it (2 Sam 5:6-9; 1 Chr 11:4-8) — the Chronicler who narrates David’s capture of Jerusalem places the Jebusite population within Noah’s grandson Canaan’s genealogy.
1.15: Hivites (ĥiwwî), Arkites (ʿarqî), Sinites (sînî). The Hivites are the people of Gibeon who deceived Joshua into a covenant (Josh 9); the covenant Joshua was tricked into became the template for Israelite treatment of Canaanites within the land. The genealogy places even Israel’s treaty-partner peoples within the Noahic framework.
1.16: Arvadites (ʾarwāḏî), Zemarites (ṣĕmārî), Hamathites (ĥămātî). Hamath is the northernmost city of the Canaanite region — ‘the entrance of Hamath’ is the standard northern boundary marker for the ideal extent of Israel’s territory (Num 34:8; 1 Kgs 8:65). The Chronicler closes the Canaan list at Hamath, bracketing the Canaanite genealogy within the promised land’s geographical limits.
1.17: Sons of Shem (bênê šêm): Elam, Asshur, Arphaxad, Lud, Aram — then Uz, Hul, Gether, and Meshech. The list in Gen 10:22 gives only five sons of Shem; the additional names (Uz, Hul, Gether, Meshech) appear in Gen 10:23 as sons of Aram, not Shem. Chronicles conflates them, placing Aram’s sons at the same level as Shem’s sons — possibly a textual transmission error or deliberate compression. The LXX of Chronicles reads similarly. This is a significant textual variant from the Genesis Vorlage: the Chronicler’s Shem-list is not a simple copy of Gen 10.
1.18: Arphaxad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber (wayyôleḏ ʾarpaḵšaḏ ʾet-šelaḥ wĕšelaḥ yālaḏ ʾet-ʿēḇer). The introduction of the wayyôleḏ verb (narrative past: ‘and he fathered’) after the name-only lists of vv5-17 signals that the Arphaxad-Shelah-Eber line is the one that matters. The genealogy narrows structurally as it approaches the covenant ancestor: verbal narration replaces paratactic names.
1.19: Eber had two sons: the first was named Peleg (peleḡ), ‘because in his time the earth was divided’ (kî bêyāmāyw niplêḡāh hāʾāreṣ). peleḡ derives from the root pālaḡ (to divide, split). The paronomasia — naming a child after the event of his era — is standard Hebrew etymological convention. The phrase niplêḡāh hāʾāreṣ (the earth was divided, Niphal perfect) is usually interpreted as the dispersal of nations after Babel (Gen 11:1-9). The Chronicler preserves this note verbatim from Gen 10:25 without elaboration.
1.20: Joktan (yoqṭān) fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah — beginning the South Arabian branch. Several names correspond to known Arabian toponyms: Hazarmaveth = Hadramaut in Yemen. The Joktan branch (vv20-23) is a closed catalogue; the genealogy does not extend it beyond one generation. The covenant path through Peleg is where the narrative interest lies.
1.21: Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah — three more Joktan sons. Uzal is usually identified with Sana’a in Yemen (cf. Ezek 27:19). The place-names embedded in the genealogy provide the Chronicler’s audience with a mental map of the known world from the vantage of the ancient Near East.
1.22: Ebal, Abimael, Sheba — more Joktan sons. Sheba appears here as a Joktan descendant (South Arabian) and also in v9 as a Cushite (through Raamah). The double appearance of Sheba in both the Ham and Shem branches reflects either competing traditions the genealogy preserves without harmonizing, or the awareness that the ‘Sheba’ toponym covered multiple geographic/ethnic entities.
1.23: Ophir, Havilah, Jobab — final Joktan sons. The closing formula kol-ʾēlleh bĕnê yoqṭān (all these were the sons of Joktan) marks the end of a genealogical sub-unit. Ophir is the gold-source to which Solomon sent trading expeditions (1 Kgs 9:28; 1 Chr 29:4 ‘gold of Ophir’). Its placement in the Table of Nations grounds the Solomonic trade network in the created human geography — the distant lands that supply the temple treasures are part of the same Noahic family.
1.24: Shem, Arphaxad, Shelah (šêm ʾarpaḵšaḏ šālaḥ) — the asyndetic list resumes after the narrative notes of vv18-23, now tracing the line directly from Shem to Abraham. The use of the same paratactic style as vv1-3 signals a vertical genealogy (linear descent) rather than a branching one (horizontal). The Shem-to-Abraham spine is the structural backbone of all OT covenantal genealogy.
1.25: Eber, Peleg, Reu (ʿēḇer peleḡ rĕʿû) — continuing the direct descent line. Eber is the ancestor of the ʿiḇrîm (Hebrews); the connection between Eber and ‘Hebrew’ was recognized in ancient tradition (Gen 10:21 ‘Shem, the father of all the children of Eber’).
1.26: Serug, Nahor, Terah (śĕrûḡ nāĥôr teraḥ) — three generations before Abraham. Terah led the family from Ur of the Chaldeans toward Canaan but stopped at Haran (Gen 11:31). The brevity of the list (one name per verse) accelerates the reader toward the climax: Abraham.
1.27: ʾaḇrām hûʾ ʾaḇrāhām — ‘Abram, that is, Abraham.’ The identifying particle hûʾ functions as an equative marker: this person Abram is the one who became Abraham (Gen 17:5, where YHWH changes the name because ‘I have made you the father of a multitude of nations’ — ʾaḇ h˘môn gôyîm, a paronomasia on ʾaḇrāhām). The Chronicler signals the name-change without retelling the covenant of circumcision (Gen 17). The hûʾ identification also appears in v34 for Israel/Jacob — the Chronicler’s shorthand for covenant identity transition.
1.28: Sons of Abraham: Isaac and Ishmael (bênê ʾaḇrāhām yiṣḥāq wĕyišmāʿêl). The order (Isaac first, Ishmael second) reverses birth order — Ishmael is the elder (Gen 16) — to signal covenantal priority. In Hebrew genealogy, first position in a list connotes primary standing. Paul makes this argument explicitly in Gal 4:22-31 (the two sons of Abraham representing the two covenants).
1.29: Ishmael’s sons ‘in birth order’ (bêḵûrôtām): the firstborn Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam. bêḵûrôt is an abstract noun from bêḵôr (firstborn) meaning ‘sequence of birth.’ The specification that Ishmael’s sons are listed in birth order emphasizes that the list is complete and properly ordered. Kedar appears in prophetic texts (Isa 21:16-17; 42:11; 60:7) as a significant North Arabian tribal group.
1.30: Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema — five more sons of Ishmael. Massa and Tema appear in Prov 30:1 and 31:1 as place-names associated with wisdom literature. The genealogy grounds the non-Israelite wisdom tradition (Proverbs draws on an international wisdom heritage) within the Abrahamic family.
1.31: Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah — final sons of Ishmael. ‘These were the sons of Ishmael’ (ʾēlleh hêm bênê yišmāʿêl) closes the Ishmael catalogue. Jetur and Naphish appear in 1 Chr 5:19-22 as peoples against whom the Transjordanian tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh fought successfully — the genealogical names in ch. 1 remain historically active in the Chronicler’s own narrative.
1.32: Sons of Keturah, Abraham’s concubine (pîlegeš): Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, Shuah. pîlegeš (concubine) is the precise term used in Gen 25:6 for Keturah’s status — secondary wife, not the primary covenant wife Sarah. Midian is the most significant: the Midianites are Israel’s enemy (Num 25; Judg 6-8), yet Moses’s father-in-law Jethro is a Midianite priest (Exod 2:16-18) — Abraham’s descendant and Israel’s adversary simultaneously.
1.33: Sons of Midian: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah. ‘All these were the descendants of Keturah’ (kol-ʾēlleh bênê qĕṭûrāh). The closing formula ends the three-branch Abraham genealogy (Ishmael / Keturah / Isaac). Ephah of Midian appears in Isa 60:6 among the nations that will bring gold and frankincense to Jerusalem — the genealogical root of Ephah in Abraham positions this future ingathering of nations as a homecoming of distant family.
1.34: wayyôleḏ ʾaḇrāhām ʾet-yiṣḥāq — ‘Abraham fathered Isaac.’ After the extensive Ishmael and Keturah branches, the covenantal line snaps back to a single statement. ‘Sons of Isaac: Esau and Israel’ (ʿêšāw wĕyiśrāʾêl) — the Chronicler uses the covenant name ‘Israel’ rather than ‘Jacob’ throughout, consistent with his practice of preferring the name given in Gen 32:28 / Gen 35:10. Esau is listed first by birth order; Israel second by covenantal priority. The same inversion as Isaac before Ishmael (v28): covenantal priority supersedes birth order throughout this genealogy.
1.35: Sons of Esau (bênê ʿêšāw): Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, Korah. Corresponding exactly to Gen 36:4-5. Eliphaz and Reuel are sons of Esau’s Canaanite and Ishmaelite wives respectively. The Esau branch receives more attention (vv35-42) than the Keturah branch, because Edom (Esau’s nation) is the Chronicler’s politically relevant neighbor in the post-exilic period.
1.36: Sons of Eliphaz (bênê ʾĕlîpaz): Teman, Omar, Zephi, Gatam, Kenaz, Timna, and Amalek. The list differs from Gen 36:12, which lists Timna as a concubine of Eliphaz who bore Amalek — making Timna a mother rather than a sibling. Chronicles places both as Eliphaz’s progeny at the same level. Amalek is the significant name: the Amalekites are Israel’s primordial enemy (Exod 17:8-16; Deut 25:17-19; 1 Sam 15), and their eponymous ancestor is here placed as Eliphaz’s son — Abraham’s great-great-grandson.
1.37: Sons of Reuel (bênê rĕʿûʾêl): Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, Mizzah — identical to Gen 36:13 (with the same names). Reuel is also the name of Moses’s Midianite father-in-law in Exod 2:18; the shared name is a coincidence of common ancient Near Eastern naming practice, not a genealogical connection.
1.38: Sons of Seir (bênê śêʿîr): Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, Dishan. These are not Esau’s sons but the original Horite inhabitants of the Seir region whom Esau displaced and intermarried with (Gen 36:20-21; Deut 2:12 ‘the Horites formerly lived in Seir’). The Chronicler includes them because the nation Israel knows as Edom is an ethnic blend of Esau’s sons and the indigenous Horite population.
1.39: Sons of Lotan (bênê lôṭān): Hori and Homam. Lotan’s sister was Timna (wĕʾăĥôt lôṭān timnāʿ). This Timna is the same woman who becomes Eliphaz’s concubine and the mother of Amalek (Gen 36:22). The Chronicler notes the relationship without comment: the intermarriage of Esau’s line with the Seirite Horites produces the Edomite nation.
1.40: Sons of Shobal (bênê šôḇāl): Alian, Manahath, Ebal, Shephi, Onam. Sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah. The Horite clans continue. Manahath appears also in 1 Chr 8:6 as a place-name in Benjamin — the genealogy’s personal names and later territorial names overlap in ways that trace Israel’s own land-distribution back to pre-Israelite inhabitants.
1.41: Anah’s son: Dishon (bênê ʿānāh dîšôn). Sons of Dishon: Hamran, Eshban, Ithran, Cheran. The list corresponds to Gen 36:26 with minor spelling differences (Hemdan/Hamran). The depth of this Horite genealogy — four generations from Seir — reflects that the Chronicler is tracking the Edomite social structure with some precision.
1.42: Sons of Ezer (bênê ʾêṣer): Bilhan, Zaavan, Jaakan. Sons of Dishan (bênê dîšān): Uz and Aran. The Ezer/Dishan branch closes the Horite catalogue. Jaakan appears in Num 33:31-32 as a wilderness camping station (Bene-jaakan) — connecting the genealogical eponyms to the physical geography of Israel’s wilderness journey.
1.43: wĕʾêlleh hammêlāḵîm ʾăšer māleḵû bêʾereṣ ʾeḏôm lipnê meloḵ-melek lêḇenê yiśrāʾêl — ‘These are the kings who reigned in Edom before any king reigned over Israel.’ The temporal clause lipnê meloḵ-melek (before a king reigned) uses the infinitive construct of mālaḵ (to reign as king) twice — the idiom marks the Edomite list as pre-Davidic. For the Chronicler, whose entire narrative centers on the Davidic monarchy, this clause is prospective: it marks the Edomite king-list as temporally prior and implicitly asks “when will Israel have its own king?” The list of Edom’s kings without a permanent dynasty sets up the contrast with the Davidic covenant’s promise of an eternal dynasty.
1.44: When Bela died, Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah reigned (wayyimloḵ taḥtāyw, ‘reigned in his place’). The succession formula taḥtāyw (lit. ‘under/in his place’) is the standard idiom for replacement-succession. What is absent is any father-son relation: Jobab is ‘son of Zerah,’ not ‘son of Bela.’ The Edomite kingship is elective or achieved, not hereditary — a stark contrast with the Davidic promise in 2 Sam 7:12 (‘I will raise up your offspring after you’) and 1 Chr 17:11-14.
1.45: When Jobab died, Husham of the land of the Temanites reigned (wayyimloḵ taḥtāyw ĥûšām mêʾereṣ hatêmānî). Geographic origins replace patronymics throughout this list: kings are identified by where they come from (Bozrah, Temanite land, Moabite territory, Masrekah, Rehoboth-by-the-River), not whose sons they are. Power rotates among regional power bases — the non-dynastic nature of Edomite kingship is built into the formula.
1.46: Hadad son of Bedad (h˘ḏaḏ ben-bêḏaḏ) reigned after Husham — ‘he had defeated Midian on the territory of Moab’ (hammaккeh ʾet-miḏyān biśĕḏêh môʾāḇ). This is the only king in the list identified by a military achievement. The mention of a Midian-defeat connects the Edomite king-list to the broader geopolitical struggles of the region — the same Midianites who descended from Keturah (v33) and who are Israel’s adversary in the wilderness period.
1.47: Samlah of Masrekah (śamllāh mimmāśrêqāh) reigned after Hadad. Masrekah is unidentified but the name suggests a wadi-region (the root śrq relates to vines or a winding course). The place-names in this list are drawn from an ancient source that the Chronicler and Gen 36 preserve verbatim; they provide a window into the political geography of Transjordan before the Israelite settlement.
1.48: Shaul of Rehoboth-by-the-River (šāʾûl mêreĥôḇôt hannāhār) reigned after Samlah. hannāhār (the River) in OT geographical language refers to the Euphrates (cf. Gen 31:21; Exod 23:31). ‘Rehoboth-by-the-Euphrates’ places this king’s origin in northern Syria — evidence that the Edomite royal selection drew from a wide geographic pool, perhaps through trade networks.
1.49: Baal-hanan son of Achbor (baʿal-ḥānān ben-ʿaḵbôr) reigned after Shaul. The theophoric name Baal-hanan (‘the Lord [Baal] is gracious’) is the only Baal-compound name in the Edomite king-list. In 1 Chr 27:28, a Baal-hanan is David’s overseer of the olive and sycamore groves in the Shephelah — possibly an Edomite or Canaanite official appointed by David, with the name surviving in administrative use.
1.50: When Baal-hanan died, Hadad reigned (wayyimloḵ taḥtāyw h˘ḏaḏ). His city was Pai (pāʿî; written Pau in Gen 36:39 — a minor spelling variant). His wife was Mehetabel daughter of Matred, daughter of Me-zahab (mêhêṭaḇʾêl bat-maṭrêḏ bat mê-zāhāḇ). The wife’s name and two-generation maternal genealogy is unusual for this list; it is the most detailed entry. mê-zāhāḇ literally means ‘waters of gold’ — possibly a gold-producing region.
1.51: Hadad also died (wayyāmāt h˘ḏaḏ) — the abrupt death notice without naming a successor signals the end of the king-list and the transition to the chief-list. The chiefs of Edom (ʾallûpê ʾeḏôm): Timna, Aliah, Jetheth. ʾallûp (chief, lit. ‘one of a thousand’) differs from melek (king) — the chiefs are clan-leaders rather than monarchs. The shift from king-list to chief-list may reflect different periods of Edomite social organization.
1.52: Chief Oholibamah (ʾallûp ʾohôlîḇāmāh), Elah, Pinon. Oholibamah is a female name (the same as Esau’s wife in Gen 36:2) used here as a chief’s name — either the same person or a place-name that became a clan designation. The use of female-associated names for clan chiefs reflects that tribal genealogical identity was often traced through prominent women.
1.53: Chief Kenaz (ʾallûp qênaz), Teman, Mibzar. Kenaz is also the name of Esau’s grandson (v36: Eliphaz’s son) and the ancestor of Othniel the Kenizzite (Josh 15:17; Judg 3:9 ‘Othniel the son of Kenaz, Caleb’s younger brother’). The Kenizzites are among the pre-Israelite peoples whose land is promised to Abraham’s descendants (Gen 15:19). The overlap between the Edomite chief Kenaz and Israelite judge Othniel’s lineage reflects the complex ethnic interweaving of Israelite and Edomite ancestry in the Negev region.
1.54: Chief Magdiel (ʾallûp magdîʾêl) and chief Iram. ‘These were the chiefs of Edom’ (ʾêlleh ʾallûpê ʾeḏôm) — the closing formula ends the Edomite section and closes chapter 1 entirely. The Chronicler has moved from Adam (v1) to the post-Abrahamic nations, demonstrating that Israel and Judah stand within, not apart from, the full human genealogical matrix. The chapter is simultaneously universal (beginning at Adam) and particular (closing on Edom, Israel’s near-neighbor and adversary) before the Chronicler turns in chapter 2 to Israel’s own tribes.

2.7: The Chronicler names Achan as ʿāḵār — not the form used in Joshua (where the name is ʿāḵān, Josh 7:1-26) — and immediately glosses it: ʿōḵēr yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer māʿal maʿal bᵉḥērem, ‘the troubler of Israel who broke faith in the matter of the devoted thing.’ The name ʿāḵār is a deliberate morphological pun on the verb ʿāḵar (to trouble, disturb, bring calamity) — the same root that appears in Josh 7:25, where Joshua says meh ʿăḵartānû YHWH yaʿkorᵉḵā hayyôm hazzeh, ‘Why did you bring trouble on us? YHWH brings trouble on you today.’ The valley of execution is subsequently named ʿēmeq ʿāḵôr, ‘Valley of Achor/Trouble’ (Josh 7:26; Hos 2:15; Isa 65:10). The Chronicler’s renaming of Achan as Achar encodes the interpretation into the name itself: the man whose act brought ʿāḵar on Israel is remembered by the name of his deed. This is the genealogy’s only explicit negative notation in the Judahite line — a single name that carries the full weight of the ḥērem-violation and its consequences. The principle that one person’s covenant-breach affects the entire community (Josh 7:1: ‘the people of Israel broke faith’) is what the Achar/ʿāḵar wordplay encodes.

3.17: The designation of Jeconiah (Jehoiachin) as hāʾăsîr — ‘the captive’ or ‘the prisoner’ — in the genealogy (v17) is the Chronicler’s recognition that the Davidic line passed through a deposed and imprisoned king. The word ʾāsîr (prisoner, captive) marks Jeconiah as the king taken to Babylon in 597 BCE (2 Kgs 24:8-16) where he remained in prison until Evil-merodach released him in 561 BCE (2 Kgs 25:27-30). The Chronicler’s post-exilic Davidic genealogy (vv17-24) continues through this prisoner — Shealtiel, Zerubbabel, and six more generations — demonstrating that the exile and imprisonment of the Davidic king did not sever the covenant line. Zerubbabel (v19) is the key post-exilic Davidic figure, governor of Judah under Persia (Hag 1:1; Zech 4:6-10) and ancestor of Jesus in Luke’s genealogy (Luke 3:27). Matt 1:11-12 includes Iechonias at the Babylon deportation as the hinge-point of the genealogy — the break and the continuity simultaneously — echoing the Chronicler’s own ambivalent designation: the ʾāsîr through whom the Messiah would come. The word ʾāsîr also carries the resonance of Isa 53:8 (meʿōṣer ûmimmišpāṭ luqqāḥ, ‘by oppression and judgment he was taken away’) — the Servant whose suffering is encompassed in the same vocabulary of imprisonment and removal.

7.23: The naming of Ephraim’s son Beriah carries a folk etymology: wayyiqrāʾ ʾet šᵉmô bᵉrîʿāh kî bᵉrāʿāh hāyᵉtāh bᵉḇêtô, ‘and he called his name Beriah because disaster had come to pass (bᵉrāʿāh hāyᵉtāh) in his house.’ The etymology is a paronomastic play: the name bᵉrîʿāh is heard as bᵉ-rāʿāh, ‘in/with calamity’ (from the root rāʿaʿ). The occasion is the death of Ephraim’s sons at the hands of the men of Gath (v21) — a genealogical fragment not attested elsewhere. The naming-in-grief practice appears throughout the patriarchal narratives: Rachel names her last son Ben-oni (‘son of my sorrow’) before Jacob renames him Benjamin (Gen 35:18); Phinehas’s wife names her son Ichabod (‘no glory’) at the loss of the ark (1 Sam 4:21). The Chronicler’s inclusion of the Beriah fragment is unusual in a genealogical list — it interrupts the sequence with a narrative moment — suggesting the Chronicler is drawing on an independent tradition about Ephraim’s household loss. Beriah’s name encodes the family history: his very existence is memorialized as born out of tragedy.

8.33: The Chronicler names Saul’s sons as Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and ʾeš baʿal (Eshbaal, ‘man of Baal’); Jonathan’s son is named mᵉrîḇ baʿal (Merib-baal, ‘Baal contends/pleads’). These are the original theophoric names. The parallel passages in Samuel use ʾîšbōšet (Ishbosheth, ‘man of shame’) and mᵉpîḇōšet (Mephibosheth, ‘from the mouth of shame’) — systematic substitutions of bōšet (shame, disgrace) for baʿal. This scribal practice of replacing Baal-element theophoric names with bōšet is attested across Samuel and reflects the later theological aversion to preserving Baal-names even in personal names from the Saul-David period. The Chronicler preserves the older forms: the theophoric baʿal in these pre-monarchic and early monarchic personal names did not necessarily indicate Baal-worship — baʿal as a common noun means ‘lord’ or ‘master’ and was sometimes used of YHWH (Hos 2:16: tiqrᵉʾî ʾîšî wᵉlōʾ tiqrᵉʾî lî ʿôḏ baʿlî, ‘you will call me “My Husband” and no longer call me “My Baal”’ — YHWH himself once called baʿal). The Samuel substitutions are a later editorial censorship; the Chronicler’s retention of Eshbaal and Merib-baal reflects either access to earlier sources or a different editorial policy. The LXX often mediates between both traditions.

4.1: The genealogy resumes the sons of Judah from a different entry point than ch2 — Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal. The formula here is bᵉnê Yᵉhûdāh (sons of Judah), the standard opening for a new genealogical unit. The five names bridge the main Judahite lines descended from ch2, grouping together diverse sub-clans under the Judahite heading.
4.2: The genealogical verb hôlîd (hiphil of yālad) means “caused to beget” or “fathered” — the standard causative form for genealogical chains. The MKT renders it “fathered” throughout, preserving the Hebrew's emphasis on the active role of the father in the chain rather than the more neutral English “was the father of.”
4.3: The formula ʾăbî (father of) appears before place names throughout this chapter — ʾăbî Ētām, “father of Etam.” In genealogical contexts, ʾāḇ before a place name connotes “founder” or “settler” of that location. The MKT renders this “founder of” to capture the sense that these individuals established settlements, not merely that they had children named after towns.
4.4: Pᵉnûʾēl ʾăbî Gᵉdōr wᵉʿEzer ʾăbî Ḥûšāh — two founder-formulas in parallel. Pᵉnûʾēl may carry the resonance of the Jabbok crossing (Gen 32:30, “face of God”), though here it is a personal name. Ḥûšāh likely corresponds to the town from which Hushai the Archite came (2 Sam 15:32). The closing attribution “sons of Hur, firstborn of Ephrathah, father of Bethlehem” identifies this line's connection to Bethlehem through Hur.
4.5: Ašḥûr ʾăbî Tᵉqôaʿ — founder of Tekoa. Tekoa was the home of Amos the prophet (Amos 1:1) and the wise woman summoned by Joab (2 Sam 14:2). The two wives, Ḥelāh and Naʿărāh, introduce a brief chiastic genealogy: Naarah's sons in v6, Helah's in v7.
4.6: The sons of Naarah are listed first, then Helah's (v7) — the order reverses normal birth-order priority, perhaps reflecting a significance judgment by the Chronicler. The names are otherwise unattested and carry no clear etymology relevant to the commentary; the list preserves clan identity records that the post-exilic community needed for land and inheritance disputes.
4.7: The sons of Helah close the Ashhur sub-unit. Yiṣhar (from yāṣhar, “to shine/be bright”) appears also as a Levitical name in Exod 6:18. The Chronicler includes both branches of Ashhur's family, giving equal genealogical standing to both wives' descendants.
4.8: Qôṣ hôlîd ʾet-ʿAnûb — Koz fathered Anub. Qôṣ (thorn) is a name that may reflect the topography of the clan's territory. Zobebah (שֹׁובֵבָה, from šûḇ, “to return”) appears to be a personal or clan name unique to this verse. The formula mišpᵉḥôt ʾAḥarḥēl (clans of Aharhel) indicates the Chronicler is working from clan-registry sources in addition to personal genealogies.
4.9: Yaʿbēṣ (Jabez) is explicitly glossed by the narrator: ʾāmᵉrāh ʾimmô qārāʾtî šᵉmô yaʿbēṣ lēʾmōr kî yāladtî bᵉʿōṣeḇ — “his mother named him Jabez, saying, 'I bore him in pain/sorrow.'” The name yaʿbēṣ inverts the letters of ʿōṣeḇ (sorrow/pain) in a folk etymology. The Chronicler introduces Jabez mid-genealogy as “more honoured than his brothers” (niḵbad miʾaḥāyw) — a signal to the reader that an exceptional figure follows.
4.10: The prayer of Jabez is dense with literary technique. ʾim-bārēḵ tᵉḇārᵉḵēnî — the infinitive absolute (bārēḵ) before the finite verb (tᵉḇārᵉḵēnî) intensifies the plea: “if you would indeed bless me.” wᵉhirbîtā ʾet-gᵉḇûlî — “enlarge my territory,” using the hiphil of rāḇāh (to be many/great). The prayer closes with lᵉḇiltî ʿoṣbî — “that I might not have pain,” returning to the ʿōṣeḇ root of his name. The prayer is thus a lifelong response to the sorrow of his birth: he asks God to write a different story than his name predicted.
4.11: Kᵉlûḇ ʾăḥî Šûḥāh hôlîd ʾet-Mᵉḥîr — the formula is straightforward. Kᵉlûḇ is a variant spelling of Kālēḇ (Caleb), which may or may not be the same figure as the spy of Numbers 13. The genealogical chain here uses the standard hôlîd causative, tracking through Mechir to Eshton in v12.
4.12: Ešṭōn hôlîd ʾet-Bêt-rāpāʾ — Eshton fathered Beth-rapha (“house of healing/giant”). Ir-Naḥāš (city of serpent/bronze) is otherwise unknown geographically but likely a Judahite settlement. ʾanšê Rēkāh (men of Recah) indicates the Chronicler is again working from guild or clan-settlement records whose names have not survived elsewhere.
4.13: bᵉnê Qᵉnaz — Kenaz was an Edomite clan chief (Gen 36:11) who became assimilated into Judah, so Othniel the Kenizzite (Judg 1:13; 3:9-11), Israel's first judge, descended from a non-Israelite ancestor. The name Othniel is possibly from ʿōz (strength) + ʾēl (God). The reference here is brief; the fuller picture comes from Judges.
4.14: Gêʾ-Ḥărāšîm — “Valley of Craftsmen,” from ḥārāš (craftsman, artisan, metalworker). The phrase kî ḥărāšîm hāyû (“for they were craftsmen”) explains the place name — a settlement of artisans in the service of the Judahite kingdom, perhaps supplying military equipment. This is one of the Chronicler's incidental socioeconomic footnotes preserved nowhere else.
4.15: bᵉnê Kālēḇ ben-Yᵉphunneh — Caleb son of Jephunneh, the spy who gave a positive report (Num 13:30; 14:6-9). This Caleb is distinct from the Calebites of v11-12 who may or may not be the same genealogical line. The name Elah (אֵלָה, terebinth tree) is a common theophoric/nature name in the HB.
4.16: The sons of Jehallelel: four names, none of whom appear elsewhere. The genealogical function is territorial — recording which sub-clans held Judahite land in the Chronicler's administrative framework. The formula bᵉnê X without the hôlîd chain indicates a horizontal listing of clan members rather than a vertical father-son chain.
4.17: The genealogy of Ezrah introduces Miriam — a female name notable in this context. The Chronicler occasionally inserts women's names into otherwise male genealogies when they were clan founders or significant mothers (cf. Shelomith in 2 Chr 11:20). Mered (rebellion) appears to have taken two wives: an Egyptian wife (v18) and a Jewish wife (v18b), giving him two distinct lines of descendants.
4.18: ʾiššātô hayᵉhûdîyyāh — “his Jewish wife” — the explicit ethnic marker “Judahite/Jewish” (yᵉhûdîyyāh) is needed because Mered had an Egyptian wife (named Bithiah/Bityah in v18, “daughter of Yah”). The name Bithiah is possibly a Hebraized form honoring a pious Egyptian woman, or it may reflect an Israelite name given at conversion — the daughter of Pharaoh identified in Exod 2:5-10 is sometimes connected with this Bithiah in rabbinic tradition.
4.19: The sons of Hodiah's wife, “sister of Naham” — Hodiah married a woman whose clan identity comes through her brother, not her father. Kᵉîlāh haGarmî (Keilah the Garmite settlement) — Garmî is of uncertain origin; possibly connected to gerem (bone/substance) as a place name. Eštemoa hamaʿăḵātî — Eshtemoa the Maachathite — the Maachathites were a people east of the Jordan (Deut 3:14), so this entry records mixed-ancestry clans within Judah.
4.20: Sons of Shimon and Ishi: a brief four-name and two-name list. Ben-Ḥānān (son of gracious one) and Ben-Zōḥēt (son of Zoheth) use ben (son of) as part of the actual name — these are not genealogical formulas but hypocoristic names that incorporate ben as a prefix, similar to Benjamin.
4.21: bᵉnê Šēlāh ben-Yᵉhûdāh — Shelah was Judah's third son (Gen 38:5), whose line was passed over at Tamar's double deception to allow Perez and Zerah to take precedence. The inclusion here rehabilitates Shelah's descendants as legitimate Judahites. Bêt-ʿăḇodāt-bûṣ — “house of linen workers” or “house of the guild of linen workers.” The ʿăḇôdāh (service/work) term applied to a textile guild suggests organized craft production, perhaps in royal service.
4.22: This verse contains a textual difficulty: wᵉyāšuḇî Lehem — the MT reads “and they returned to Lehem (Bethlehem).” The LXX reads differently; some scholars emend to a place name, others retain a verb meaning “returned.” The note “the records are ancient” (wᵉhaddᵉḇārîm ʿattîqîm) is the Chronicler's own acknowledgment that his sources for this material are antiquarian — he is working from old clan registers, not living memory.
4.23: hāyôṣᵉrîm — “the potters” (from yāṣar, to form/shape, the same root used for YHWH forming man in Gen 2:7). The phrase yōšᵉḇê-šām ʿim-hammelek bimlāʾktô — “they dwelt there in the king's service” — indicates royal workshops. The Chronicler preserves evidence of royal craft guilds operating under patronage from a Judahite king, a practice well-attested in ANE administrative texts.
4.24: bᵉnê Šimʿôn — the shift to Simeon's genealogy is significant. Simeon received no separate tribal territory in Canaan but was absorbed within Judah's allotment (Josh 19:1-9). The Chronicler's inclusion of Simeon validates their continued existence as a tribe even within Judah's geographic territory. The five names here differ slightly from Gen 46:10 and Num 26:12-14 — normal variation in genealogical transmission.
4.25: The tôlᵉdôt chain for Simeon (Shallum, Mibsam, Mishma) uses the standard linear formula: bᵉnô X bᵉnô Y — “his son X, his son Y” — indicating a direct father-to-son chain rather than the sibling-list of v24. Mibsam also appears as an Ishmaelite name (Gen 25:13), suggesting a possible tribal overlap or shared naming tradition.
4.26: The chain continues through Mishma's descendants. bᵉnê Mišmāʿ — sons of Mishma — then lists three consecutive bᵉnô links: Hamuel, Zaccur, Shimei. This is the deepest Simeonite genealogy preserved in the HB and likely drawn from clan records maintained through the Assyrian and Babylonian periods.
4.27: Šimʿî šiššāh-ʿāśār bānîm wᵉšēš bānôt — Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters. The unusual female count (bānôt = daughters) is recorded here and again stands out in the genealogical framework. The theological note follows: “his brothers did not multiply greatly, and their whole clan did not equal the sons of Judah.” The comparison to Judah frames the Simeonite genealogy as an explanation for Simeon's absorption into Judah — smaller population means less territorial claim.
4.28: The settlement list for Simeon: Beer-sheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual — cities at the southern edge of Judah's territory, in the Negev. Beer-sheba was the southernmost city of the traditional settlement (“from Dan to Beer-sheba”). The phrase establishes the Simeonites as desert-edge settlers rather than highland farmers like Judah.
4.29: Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad — southern Negev settlements listed in Josh 15:29-30 and 19:3-4 as Judahite and Simeonite towns respectively. The Chronicler's list slightly diverges from Joshua in names and spelling, reflecting either textual transmission variation or different administrative periods of settlement.
4.30: Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag — Hormah (ḥormāh, “devoted to destruction”) was where Israel was routed after the failed Kadesh incursion (Num 14:45; 21:3). Ziklag became David's base during his time with the Philistines (1 Sam 27:6; 30:1). The Chronicler's inclusion of Ziklag in the Simeonite list establishes the tribal claim that David later inherited by grant.
4.31: ʿad-mᵉlōk Dāwîd — “until the time of David” — a chronological marker establishing the antiquity of these settlement records. The Chronicler is saying that these were the Simeonite cities prior to the Davidic reorganization of tribal territories. The formula “these were their cities” closes the settlement list formally.
4.32: Five ḥăṣērôt (villages/enclosures) are listed. The Hebrew ḥāṣēr (pl. ḥăṣērôt) typically denotes unwalled settlements — hamlets or encampments — in contrast to fortified ʿārîm (cities). The Simeonite settlements were thus smaller, unwalled communities in the semi-arid southern zone.
4.33: ûmišpaḥōtām (and their genealogical records) — the formal close of the Simeonite list with the phrase “they maintained their genealogical records” (yiḥaśśᵉmû lāhem). This is the Chronicler's notation that these clan lists come from official genealogical registers (yaḥas, genealogy/registration), not oral tradition.
4.34: A new list begins with Meshobab and eleven additional Simeonite leaders. The transition from settlement list to leader list reflects different source materials: vv28-33 came from a geographic-administrative record; vv34-43 come from a clan-leadership register. The leaders' names are given with patronymics to several generations depth, indicating their importance.
4.35: wᵉYôʾēl wᵉYēhûʾ ben-Yôšiḇyāh ben-Śᵉrāyāh ben-ʿĂśîʾēl — a four-generation patronymic for Jehu, establishing his distinguished ancestry within the Simeonite leadership. The depth of genealogy (four generations) signals significant status; ordinary clan members warranted only one or two generations of identification.
4.36: Seven more names in the Simeonite leadership roster. ʾĔlyôʿênay (my eyes are toward YHWH) is a theophoric name reflecting post-exilic piety; its presence in this list suggests the register was updated or supplemented during the restoration period when such names became common.
4.37: Zîzāʾ ben-Šipî ben-ʾAllôn ben-Yᵉdāyāh ben-Šimrî ben-Šᵉmaʿyāh — six-generation patronymic for Ziza, the deepest in this section. Six generations of tracing suggests either an exceptionally prominent ancestor or a clan that took great pride in establishing its genealogical credentials in the post-exilic period.
4.38: habbāʾîm bᵉšēmôt — “those listed by name” — a formal designation for officially registered clan heads. The phrase wᵉnāśîʾîm bᵉmišpᵉḥōtām (leaders in their clans) establishes their administrative role. wᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtêhem pāraṣ lārōḇ — their families multiplied greatly — the expansion of Simeon in the following verses is presented as blessing (the verb pāraṣ, “to burst forth/spread,” is used of rapid growth, as in Gen 28:14 for Jacob's descendants).
4.39: wayyēlᵉkû lᵉmôḇôʾ Gᵉdōr — “they journeyed to the entrance of Gedor.” The location is uncertain — possibly Gerar (reading with LXX) in Philistine territory. lirʿôt migrᵉhem — “to find pasture for their flocks” — the motive is pastoral expansion, not military aggression. The Simeonite movement is framed as a search for grazing land, not conquest.
4.40: wayyimṣᵉʾû mirʿeh šāmēn wāṭôḇ — “they found rich, good pasture” — the language echoes the description of the Promised Land (Deut 8:7-9). šeqeṭet wĕšalᵉwāh — “peaceful and quiet” — the two adjectives together describe ideal pastoral conditions. miḥām (Ham) as the former inhabitants signals Canaanite origin; the displacement of Ham's descendants for Israel's tribes echoes the original conquest narrative.
4.41: hakkᵉtûḇîm bᵉšēmôt — “listed by name” (same formula as v38) — specifying these are the officially registered Simeonite leaders. The campaign is dated to Hezekiah king of Judah, providing a synchronization point with the Judean royal chronology. wayyakkûm (they struck them down) and wayyaḥᵉrîmûm (they devoted them to destruction) — the ḥērem verb (ḥāram in hiphil) applies the holy-war ban to the surviving Canaanite enclaves in the Negev.
4.42: ḥămiššāh meʾôt ʾîš — five hundred men, a smaller raiding party for the Mount Seir campaign. The four leaders (Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, Uzziel) are named; they are sons of Ishi (cf. v20). Mount Seir was Edomite territory (Gen 32:3; 36:8), suggesting Simeonite expansion eastward as well as southward, filling the vacuum left by Assyrian disruption of established populations.
4.43: wayyakkû ʾet-šᵉʾērît happālēṭāh laʿămālēq — “they struck down the remaining Amalekites who had escaped.” Saul had failed to complete the destruction of Amalek (1 Sam 15); David had driven them back (1 Sam 27:8; 30); now Simeonite clan warriors complete what royal campaigns had not. wayyēšᵉḇû šām ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — “they have lived there to this day” — the narrator's present-time anchor for the Simeonite settlement in Edomite territory.

5.1: hûʾ habᵉḵôr — Reuben was the firstborn (bᵉḵôr), yet the birthright (bᵉḵōrāh) was not his to keep. The indictment is precise: bᵉḥalᵉlô yᵉṣûʿê ʾāḇîw — “when he defiled his father's couch/bed.” ḥillēl (piel of ḥālal) means to profane, desecrate, or defile — the same root as ḥōl (common/profane) as opposed to qādôš (holy). The act (Gen 35:22, sleeping with Bilhah) is not described but its legal consequence is: the bᵉḵōrāh was transferred to Joseph's sons.
5.2: The Chronicler articulates a distinction between two aspects of the firstborn's inheritance: haśśᵉrārāh — the ruler/prince role — went to Judah (nagîd in some translations; here the term śar = prince/chief), while the bᵉḵōrāh (birthright = double inheritance share) went to Joseph. This two-track resolution explains the parallel importance of Judah (the royal tribe) and Joseph (the most numerous tribe) in the later history: neither invalidates the other because they received different dimensions of the firstborn's rights.
5.3: The standard list of Reuben's four sons matches Gen 46:9 and Exod 6:14 closely. Ḥanôḵ, Pallûʾ, Ḥeṣrôn, wᵉKarmî — the genealogical formula resumes after the theological aside of vv1-2. The bᵉnê Rᵉʾûḇên bᵉḵōr Yiśrāʾēl opening restates Reuben's formal position even as the narrative has explained why that position carries no privilege here.
5.4: The chain from Joel continues through eight generations (bᵉnô X — his son X, seven times). The chain uses the possessive suffix construction (bᵉnô = his son) to mark direct father-son descent, distinguishing it from the lateral bᵉnê (sons of) lists. Joel's line is the one that reaches Beerah who went into exile (v6), suggesting Joel's family was the most prominent Reubenite clan in the Assyrian period.
5.5: The chain continues: bᵉnô Mîḵāh bᵉnô Rᵉʾāyāh bᵉnô Baʿal. The name Baʿal in an Israelite genealogy is not unusual in the pre-monarchic and early monarchic period (cf. Eshbaal/Ishbosheth; Merib-baal/Mephibosheth) — baʿal simply meant “lord/master” and was applied to YHWH before the prophets specifically condemned its identification with the Canaanite deity.
5.6: ûḇᵉʾērāh bᵉnô ʾăšer hᵉglāh Tilgat-pilneser melek ʾAššûr — Beerah son of Baal, whom Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria deported. The name Tilgat-pilneser (Tiglatpileser) is a Hebraized form of the Akkadian Tukulti-apil-Ešarra (my trust is in the son of Esharra), reflecting the Assyrian throne name. The Chronicler specifies he was nāśîʾ laRᵉʾûḇēnî — a leader/prince of the Reubenites — explaining why his deportation is historically significant; removing the chief destabilized the whole tribe.
5.7: wᵉʾeḥāyw lᵉmišpᵉḥōtāyw — his kinsmen/relatives, listed by clan. The phrase bᵉhityaḥaśśām lᵉtôlᵉdôtām — “enrolled by genealogy in their generations” — specifies the administrative process: official genealogical registration (hitpael of yāḥas, to enroll by genealogy). Yᵉʾîʾēl hārōʾš wᵉZᵉḵaryāhû — Jeiel the chief (head) and Zechariah, the two senior Reubenite leaders.
5.8: wûḇelaʿ ben-ʿAzaz ben-Šema ben-Yôʾēl — Bela with a three-generation patronymic, connecting him to the same Joel line as Beerah (v6). He “settled in Aroer as far as Nebo and Baal-meon” — Aroer was on the Arnon river (Deut 2:36), Nebo was the mountain from which Moses viewed Canaan (Deut 34:1), and Baal-meon was a Moabite town (Num 32:38). Bela's clan held the core Reubenite territory east of the Jordan.
5.9: wĕlimdᵉmāʿ nāšāʾ midbārāh — “he settled eastward to the edge of the desert”, reaching as far as the Euphrates (hanNāhār = the River, a Hebrew idiom for the Euphrates). kî miknᵉhem rāḇû bᵉʾereṣ Gilʿād — the reason given is the growth of their livestock requiring expanded territory. The Chronicler frames territorial expansion as a natural consequence of blessing (population/livestock growth), not as military aggression.
5.10: ûḇimê Šāʾûl — in the days of Saul: a chronological anchor for the first Reubenite military engagement, the war against the Hagrites (Hagrîʾîm). The Hagrites were a pastoral people of the Syro-Arabian steppe, possibly connected to Hagar/Hagarites (Ps 83:6). wayyipᵉlû bᵉyāḏām — “were defeated” (lit. fell by their hands) — using the standard military defeat idiom. wayyēšᵉḇû bᵉʾohalêhem — they settled in their tents/encampments, occupying the Hagrite territory.
5.11: ûḇᵉnê Gāḏ lᵉnegeḏ šāḵᵉnû — the sons of Gad lived “opposite them” (lit. “facing them”), in Bashan. Bāšān was the fertile northern plateau east of the Sea of Galilee, famous for its cattle (Deut 32:14; Ps 22:12). The Gadites held the southern portion of Transjordan while the Reubenites held the central and northern zones. ʿad Śalkāh — to Salecah, the easternmost limit of Bashan (Deut 3:10), marking the extent of Gadite settlement.
5.12: The Gadite leadership roster: Yôʾēl hārōʾš wĕŠāpām hamišneh — Joel the chief and Shapham the second-in-command. The term hamišneh (the second, the deputy) is an administrative title used throughout Chronicles for the second-ranking officer in a hierarchy (cf. 2 Chr 28:7 for the deputy king). This shows the Chronicler working from administrative rosters, not just genealogical records.
5.13: Seven Gadite clan members listed — “their relatives by family” (ûʾăḥêhem lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtêhem). The number seven is complete and is perhaps intentional as a formulaic grouping. Miḵāʾēl, Mᵉšullām, Šeḇaʿ, Yôray, Yāḵān, Zîʿāʾ, wᵉʿEḇer šiḇʿāh — seven in all, as the text confirms.
5.14: ʾēlleh bᵉnê ʾĂḇîḥayil ben-Ḥûrî — these are sons of Abihail son of Huri, then traced through six generations: Huri, Jaroah, Gilead, Michael, Jeshishai, Jahdo, and Buz. The depth of genealogy (six ancestral links) establishes the family's antiquity and prominence in the Gadite tribal structure, comparable to the deep Simeonite pedigrees in ch4.
5.15: Aḥî ben-ʿAḇdîʾēl ben-Gûnî rōʾš lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtām — Ahi son of Abdiel son of Guni, head of their father's house. The three-generation patronymic for Ahi establishes him as the senior Gadite clan head. rōʾš lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtām (head of the father's house) is the standard administrative term for the leading patriarch of an extended family unit (bêt ʾāḇ).
5.16: wayyēšᵉḇû bᵉGilʿād bᵉḇāšān ûḇibnōtêhā — they lived in Gilead, in Bashan and its towns (bᵉnôt = daughter-towns, i.e., dependent villages). ûḇᵉkol-migrᵉšê Šārôn ʿal-tôṣᵉʾōtām — and throughout the pasturelands of Sharon to their borders. The Sharon mentioned here is likely not the coastal Sharon plain but a Transjordanian region of the same name (cf. the plural mentions in 2 Chr 4:16 of LXX; Isa 33:9).
5.17: kullām hitya_ḥasᵉsû bîmê Yôtām melek-Yᵉhûdāh ûḇîmê Yārāḇᵉʿām melek-Yiśrāʾēl — all were enrolled by genealogy during the reigns of Jotham king of Judah and Jeroboam king of Israel. This provides a double synchronization: Jotham (southern) and Jeroboam II (northern), both reigning approximately 782–740 BCE. The Chronicler dates his genealogical source to this period — a time of relative prosperity when administrative census-taking was feasible.
5.18: bᵉnê-Rᵉʾûḇên wᵉGāḏ waḥăṣî šēḇeṭ Mᵉnašše — the three Transjordanian tribes act as a military coalition. ʾanšê ḥayil (men of valor/strength) — the standard Chronicler term for capable warriors. The description of military capability is detailed: they carry shield and sword, draw the bow, and are trained in war (lāmᵉdê milḥāmāh). The census-like enumeration (“forty-four thousand seven hundred and sixty, ready for service”) reflects the administrative precision of a military muster roll.
5.19: wayyilaḥămû ʿim-haHagrîʾîm wᵉYᵉṭûr wᵉNāpîš wᵉNôḏāḇ — the coalition fought against the Hagrites, Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. Jetur and Naphish are sons of Ishmael (Gen 25:15; 1 Chr 1:31), identifying the enemies as Ishmaelite desert tribes. Nodab is otherwise unknown. The alliance of Ishmaelite and Hagrite (possibly descendants of Hagar) groups represents the primary pastoral-nomadic competition for the Transjordanian steppe.
5.20: wayyēʿāzᵉrû ʿălêhem — “they were helped against them” — the niphal of ʿāzar (to help), implying divine assistance. The reason is explicit: kî ḥāʿăqû ʾel-hāʾĕlōhîm bammilḥāmāh — “for they cried out to God in the battle.” wayyēʿāter lāhem — “and he granted their plea” — the niphal of ʿātar (to pray/entreat), meaning to be entreated, to respond to prayer. The causal chain is theological: prayer → divine response → victory.
5.21: The spoil is enumerated in detail: 50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep, 2,000 donkeys, 100,000 persons. The numbers follow the convention of large-scale military victory accounts in Chronicles, where enumeration signals completeness of triumph. The comparison with similar totals in tribal conquest narratives (Num 31:32-35) shows the Chronicler drawing on a consistent literary pattern for describing total victory.
5.22: kî mēʾĕlōhîm hammilḥāmāh — “for the war was from God” — a theological attribution formula distinctive to the Chronicler. In parallel passages in Samuel-Kings, divine causality is present but often less explicit; Chronicles surfaces it as the primary explanation. wayyēšᵉḇû taḥtêhem — “they settled in their territory” — occupying the Hagrite steppe lands until the exile (haggôlāh), which ended the Transjordanian settlements.
5.23: ûḇᵉnê ḥăṣî šēḇeṭ Mᵉnaššeh — the half-tribe of Manasseh, the third Transjordanian tribe. mippārān ʿad Baʿal-ḥermôn wĕŠᵉnîr wĕhar Ḥermôn — from Bashan northward to Baal-hermon, Senir, and Mount Hermon. Hermon (also called Senir by the Amorites, Deut 3:9) was the northern limit of territory taken from Og of Bashan, establishing the extent of Manassite control.
5.24: Seven Manassite clan heads listed as ʾanšê ḥayil ʾanšê šēmōt — “men of valor, men of renown” — a double honor title that echoes the description of David's mighty men (ch11). The use of the same heroic vocabulary links the Transjordanian chiefs to the idealized warrior tradition of the Davidic era.
5.25: wayyimʿᵃlû ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇōtêhem — “they were unfaithful to the God of their fathers” — the verb māʿal (to act unfaithfully/treacherously) is the Chronicler's key term for covenant violation that brings divine judgment (used extensively throughout Chronicles; cf. 2 Chr 26:16; 28:19, 22; 29:6; 36:14). wayyiznû — “and prostituted themselves” — zānāh (to commit sexual immorality) in the metaphorical sense of covenant apostasy, following Hosea's influential use of the marriage metaphor for YHWH-Israel relations (Hos 1:2; 4:12).
5.26: wayyāʿer ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾet-rûaḥ Pûl melek-ʾAššûr — “the God of Israel stirred up (ʿûr hiphil) the spirit (rûaḥ) of Pul king of Assyria.” The verb ʿûr (to rouse, stir, incite) is used throughout Chronicles for divine initiative — YHWH rouses kings and peoples to accomplish his purposes (2 Chr 21:16; 36:22-23). Pul is Tiglath-pileser III's throne name used in Babylonian records (2 Kgs 15:19). The identification hûʾ Tilgat-pilneser (that is, Tiglath-pileser) is the Chronicler's own gloss explaining the two names for the same king. wayyiglēm — “he exiled them” — the Reubenites, Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh were deported to Halah, Habor, Hara, and the river Gozan, the standard Assyrian exile destinations (also named in 2 Kgs 17:6 for the northern exile).

13.11: The place-name pereṣ ʿuzzāh (‘Breach of Uzzah’) that David gives to the threshing floor of Chidon encodes a double irony. The name ʿuzzāh derives from the root ʿōz (strength, might) — the man’s name literally means ‘YHWH’s strength’ or simply ‘strength.’ Uzzah is killed at the moment of deploying that strength: reaching out (wayyišlaḥ yādô) to steady the Ark. The verb pāraṣ (to breach, break out, burst forth) — wayyippeṣ bô ʾelōhîm, ‘God broke out against him’ — names the violent, boundary-crossing force of divine holiness when it encounters unauthorized contact. The pereṣ (breach) that kills Uzzah is the Ark asserting its own holiness against human management. The same verb pāraṣ appears in 14:11 (YHWH breaks through David’s enemies = Baal-perazim) and 15:13 (David’s explanation of the Uzzah incident: pāraṣ bānû YHWH, ‘YHWH broke out against us’). The Chronicler deliberately uses this root across all three chapters to create a theological signature: pāraṣ is YHWH’s characteristic verb — he breaks boundaries, whether against enemies (14:11) or against unauthorized approach (13:11). The Ark-cart episode is not merely a liturgical error; it is a failure to reckon with the pāraṣ-character of YHWH’s holiness.

14.11: David’s name for the site of his victory over the Philistines — baʿal pᵉrāṣîm, ‘Lord of breakings-through/breaches’ — uses the same root pāraṣ that appeared in 13:11 (‘Perez-uzzah’) and will reappear in 15:13. David’s own gloss is: pāraṣ ʾelōhîm ʾet ʾōyᵉḇay bᵉyāḏî kᵉpereṣ mayim, ‘God has broken through my enemies by my hand like a breaking-through of water.’ The image is a flash flood — the pereṣ mayim (water-breach) being the sudden, irresistible bursting of a dam or a wadi in flood. The Philistines’ gods are abandoned on the field (v12) and burned — the decisive reversal from Eben-ezer (1 Sam 4) where the Ark was captured by the Philistines. Now the Ark is safely housed in Jerusalem (ch13 having failed; ch15 will succeed) and YHWH’s pāraṣ falls on the Philistines instead. The Chronicler’s placement of the Baal-perazim victory between the two Ark-procession accounts (ch13: failure; ch15: success) is a theological bracket: the same God who broke out against Uzzah (for inappropriate approach) breaks through the Philistines (for encroachment on Israel). Pāraṣ is not capricious — it is YHWH’s consistent boundary-enforcement, whether the boundary being crossed is ritual or national.

15.13: David’s retrospective explanation of the Uzzah disaster — kî lōʾ dᵉrašnûhû kammišpāṭ, ‘because we did not seek him according to the ordinance’ — introduces the specific legal failure: the Ark was carried on a cart (ʿāgālāh, 13:7) rather than on poles by Levites (Num 4:15; 7:9). The verb pāraṣ appears again: pāraṣ bānû YHWH ʾelōhênû, ‘YHWH our God broke out against us.’ The Chronicler’s use of pāraṣ in all three chapters (13:11; 14:11; 15:13) is structural: the Uzzah incident (unauthorized pāraṣ) is explained by David in 15:13 using the same vocabulary, with the Baal-perazim victory (authorized pāraṣ) sandwiched between as a theological contrast. The Philistines’ gods were ‘carried away’ (nāśāʾ) and burned (14:12) — the Ark must be ‘carried’ (nāśāʾ) by the Levites (15:2, 15) on poles, not transported like cargo. The linguistic echo is deliberate: the verb nāśāʾ (to carry, lift, bear) governs both episodes, distinguishing the proper bearing of the Ark from the improper transport. The kammišpāṭ (‘according to the ordinance’) that David names as missing is the Mosaic regulation the Ark-cart circumvented — the failure was not ignorance but inattentiveness to the specific form YHWH had ordained.

11.1: ʿaṣmᵉḵā ûḇāśārᵉḵā (‘your bone and your flesh’) is the OT’s covenant-kinship formula (Gen 2:23 Adam of Eve; Judg 9:2; 2 Sam 5:1). The elders appeal to shared descent to legitimate David’s kingship over all Israel after Saul’s collapse — the same formula the parallel passage (2 Sam 5:1) uses. Chronicles omits all of David’s Hebron years and Ishbosheth’s rival reign, moving immediately to this moment of national unity.
11.2: ʾattāh hûʾ hammôṣîʾ wᵉhammēḇîʾ (‘you were the one who led out and brought in Israel’) — military idiom for commanding an army (Num 27:17; 1 Sam 18:13). The elders recall David’s past military leadership under Saul as grounds for accepting him as king — he was the real commander all along.
11.3: wayyikrōt dāwid lāhem bᵉrît biḥeḇrôn lipnê YHWH — David cuts a covenant with the elders ‘before the LORD’ at Hebron. The Davidic kingship is established as a covenant act witnessed by YHWH, not merely a political agreement. kᵉḏiḇar YHWH bᵉyaḏ šᵉmûʾēl — ‘according to the word of YHWH by the hand of Samuel’ — Chronicles frames the event as prophetic fulfillment from the start.
11.4: yᵉrûšālaim hiʾ yᵉḇûs — ‘Jerusalem, that is Jebus’ — Chronicles identifies Jerusalem by its pre-conquest name (Judges 19:10-11). The Jebusites had held the city since the conquest. The Chronicler begins David’s reign with the capture of YHWH’s eventual throne city.
11.5: lōʾ tāḇôʾ hēnnāh — ‘you shall not come in here’ — the Jebusites’ taunt; in 2 Sam 5:6 they add ‘even the blind and the lame will ward you off’. Chronicles omits the taunt content and focuses on the outcome: wayyilkaḏ dāwid ʾet maṣṣaḏat ṣiyyôn — ‘David captured the stronghold of Zion’ — establishing the theological center of the Davidic kingdom.
11.6: The appointment formula: kol makkēh yᵉḇûsî bārîʾšônāh yihyeh lᵉrōʾš wᵉlᵉśār — ‘whoever strikes the Jebusites first shall become head and commander.’ This is a meritocratic military appointment. Joab (ben-ṣᵉrûyāh — always identified by his mother, suggesting known status) wins the position. The parallel in 2 Sam 5:8 is obscure; Chronicles’ version is clearer about the merit-based appointment.
11.7: miṣṣaḏāh dāwid — ‘from the stronghold, David [dwelt]’ — the fortress that had been Jebus becomes the ‘City of David.’ The renaming establishes the theological and dynastic identity of Jerusalem. The Chronicler uses this transition as the foundation of the temple-building narrative that dominates chs. 22–29.
11.8: Joab is credited with repairing sheʾār hāʿîr (‘the rest of the city’) while David builds from the Millo inward. hammillōʾ is likely a terraced fill structure supporting the city expansion (cf. 2 Sam 5:9). The collaboration of king and general in urban construction shows the Chronicler’s interest in organized communal building — anticipating the temple project.
11.9: wayyēlek dāwid hālôḵ wᵉḡāḏôl wᵉYHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt ʿimmô — ‘David kept going and growing, and the LORD of hosts was with him.’ The divine-warrior formula (YHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt = LORD of armies) grounds David’s expansion not in military genius but in divine presence. This is the Chronicler’s theological explanation for all Davidic success.
11.10: rāʾšê haggibborîm — ‘chiefs of the mighty men’ — introduces the famous warrior roster. ʾăšer ḥizzᵉqû-ʿimmô ḇamlûḵātô — ‘who strengthened themselves with him in his kingship’ — the Chronicler frames the Mighty Men as co-participants in establishing the Davidic kingdom, consistent with his overall emphasis on unified all-Israel support for David.
11.11: jashobeam ben-ḥakmonî — head of the three (or thirty, depending on text). The phrase rōʾš haššālišîm is disputed: ‘chief of the thirty’ (MT šᵉlōšîm) vs ‘chief of the three’ (LXX and some MSS šᵉlōšāh); the parallel in 2 Sam 23:8 has ‘Josheb-basshebeth’ a different name for possibly the same person. His feat: slaying 300 with a single spear-thrust. The number is symbolic of overwhelming martial prowess.
11.12: ʾeleʿāzār ben-dôdô haʾăḥōḥî — Eleazar son of Dodo the Ahohite, ‘one of the three mighty men.’ His story follows in v. 13-14. The Ahohites were a Benjaminite sub-clan (8:4). These three men are haggibborîm at the highest tier — an elite within the elite.
11.13: The battle at Pas-dammim (here; 2 Sam 23:11 has Lehi) where the Israelites fled from the Philistines over a barley field. The geographical difference between the parallel accounts may reflect different battle sites or text corruption. wayyāqōmû bᵉtôḵ haḥelqāh wayyaṣṣîlûhā — ‘they took their stand in the middle of the field and saved it’ — collective action; v. 14 shows it was Eleazar specifically.
11.14: wayyaṣṣîlem YHWH tᵉšûʿāh gᵉḏôlāh — ‘and YHWH saved [them] with a great salvation.’ The military victory is attributed entirely to YHWH. tᵉšûʿāh (salvation/deliverance) is the same word-root as yēšûaʿ (Joshua/Jesus). This divine-salvation formula in battle contexts establishes the theological register of all Davidic warfare.
11.15: Three of the Thirty go to David at the cave of Adullam while the Philistines are encamped in the valley of Rephaim. The rock (haṣṣûr) is identified with Adullam. The detail that maṣṣaḇ pᵉlištîm bᵉḇêt leḥem — ‘the Philistine garrison was at Bethlehem’ — marks how completely the enemy had occupied David’s home territory, heightening the poignancy of his longing.
11.16: David is ‘in the stronghold’ (bammᵉṣûḏāh) while Philistine neṣîḇ (garrison/prefect) holds Bethlehem. The contrast between the cave-king and the occupied homeland generates the narrative tension that leads to David’s thirst and its extraordinary fulfillment.
11.17: mî yašqēnî mayim mibôr bêt-leḥem — ‘Who will give me water to drink from the well of Bethlehem at the gate?’ This is an offhand expression of longing, not a command — which makes the three warriors’ response more extraordinary. The well at the gate was a civic landmark; the gate = the place of legal assembly and public life.
11.18: The three break through the Philistine garrison, draw water from Bethlehem’s well, and bring it to David. David’s response: wayyissᵉḵēhû lAYHWH — ‘he poured it out to YHWH.’ This is a libation — treating the water as too holy to drink because it was obtained at the risk of blood. The act transforms water into a sacred offering, converting an act of loyalty into an act of worship.
11.19: ḥālîlāh lî mēʾĕlōhay — ‘God forbid it of me / my God, may I not do this.’ ḥālîlāh is a strong profanation-avowal formula (Gen 44:7; Josh 22:29; 1 Sam 12:23). hᵉdām hāʾănāšîm hāʾēlleh bᵉnapšôtām — ‘the blood of these men at the cost of their lives (napšôtām).’ David refuses to consume what was purchased at the risk of life — a profound meditation on value, sacrifice, and the sacred cost of devotion.
11.20: ʾăḇîšay ʾăḥî yôʾāḇ — Abishai brother of Joab. He ‘lifted up his spear against three hundred and killed them.’ He is among haššᵉlōšāh (the three) but his status is complex: v. 21 says he was ‘more honored than the two’ (than Eleazar and Jashobeam? or the other two of the second set of three?). The text is difficult; the Chronicler seems to distinguish two tiers of three.
11.21: min-haššᵉlōšāh nikkāḇāḏ — ‘more honored than the three’ — but Abishai ‘did not attain to the first three’ (wᵉʾel-haššᵉlōšāh lōʾ-bāʾ). He was śar (officer/captain) of the three. The hierarchical ranking within the Mighty Men reflects a formal military structure.
11.22: bᵉnāyāhû ben-yᵉhôyāḏāʿ ben-ʾîš-ḥayil — ‘Benaiah son of Jehoiada, son of a valiant man.’ His three exploits: (1) slaying two ʾărîʾēl of Moab (lion-like men or ‘altar-hearths’ of Moab — meaning debated); (2) slaying a lion in a pit on a snowy day (bᵉyôm haššāleg); (3) slaying a large Egyptian (v. 23). These are listed in ascending difficulty.
11.23: Benaiah kills an Egyptian ‘a man of measure’ (ʾîš middāh — very tall, same formula as Goliath, 1 Chr 20:6) with the Egyptian’s own spear. wayyigzōl ʾet haḥănît miyyaḏ hammiṣrî wayyahaṣṣᵉhû baḥănîtô — ‘he wrested the spear from the Egyptian’s hand and killed him with his own spear.’ The reversal motif (turning an enemy’s weapon against him) appears also in David vs. Goliath and has symbolic resonance: the victor uses the enemy’s own power for his destruction. [Existing entry preserved]
11.24: ʾēlleh ʿāśāh bᵉnāyāhû — ‘these things Benaiah did’ — formal summary of v. 22-23. wayyᵉhî-lô šēm bᵉšᵉlōšāh haggibborîm — ‘he had a name among the thirty mighty men’ (MT ‘three’ — text uncertain as at v. 11). His fame is specifically named, not just assumed.
11.25: Benaiah ‘was honored beyond the thirty’ (nikkāḇāḏ hûʾ min-haššᵉlōšîm) but did not attain to the Three. David set him over his bodyguard (mišmaʿtô). This administrative note shows the Chronicler’s interest in the organization of David’s court — which directly relates to his preparations for temple governance.
11.26: The roster of ‘valiant men of the armies’ (gibbôrê haḥăyālîm) begins. These are the Thirty (roughly) — a second tier below the Three. ʾăśāhʾēl ʾăḥî yôʾāḇ — Asahel brother of Joab, who famously died in 2 Sam 2 pursuing Abner. His inclusion here, alongside Elhanan of Bethlehem, begins a list that will end with Uriah the Hittite (v. 41).
11.27: Shammoth the Harorite and Helez the Pelonite — geographical epithets identify their tribal origins. The parallel in 2 Sam 23:25-26 has slightly variant names (Shammah the Harodite / Helez the Paltite). The differences likely reflect copyist variants in proper names, a common phenomenon in these lists.
11.28: Ira son of Ikkesh the Tekoite and Abiezer the Anathothite. Anathoth is the priestly town later famous as Jeremiah’s birthplace (Jer 1:1). The warrior list crosses geographic lines — Tekoa, Anathoth, and many other towns — showing David’s coalition of all-Israel support.
11.29: Sibbecai the Hushathite — who will appear in ch. 20:4 killing Sippai the giant. Ilai the Ahohite. The Hushathites are a Judahite sub-group (1 Chr 4:4). The recurrence of Ahohite warriors (cf. v. 12) suggests a clan with a strong martial tradition.
11.30: Maharai the Netophathite and Heled son of Baanah the Netophathite. Netophah is near Bethlehem (Ezra 2:22); the two Netophathites together suggest geographic clustering within the list.
11.31: Ithai son of Ribai of Gibeah — Gibeah is Saul’s hometown (1 Sam 10:26), so this Benjaminite warrior serves David even from the house of Saul’s city. Benaiah the Pirathonite (Ephraim, Judg 12:15). The list’s geographic breadth — from Gibeah to Pirathon — reflects all-Israel participation.
11.32: Hurai of the brooks of Gaash and Abiel the Arbathite. ‘Brooks of Gaash’ references the area where Joshua was buried (Josh 24:30). The Arbathite is from Beth-arabah (Josh 15:6), near the Dead Sea rift.
11.33: Azmaveth the Baharumite (2 Sam 23:31 has ‘Barhumite’ — likely variant of the same place, Bahurim, 2 Sam 3:16) and Eliahba the Shaalbonite (from Shaalabbin, Dan’s territory, Josh 19:42). Both names appear only in the Mighty Men lists.
11.34: Jonathan son of Shage the Hararite and ‘the sons of Hashem the Gizonite’ — Gizon is otherwise unattested in the OT; possibly a scribal corruption. The Hararites appear several times in the list (vv. 34-35), suggesting a warrior clan from the hill country.
11.35: Ahiam son of Sacar the Hararite and Eliphal son of Ur. The 2 Sam 23:33 parallel has ‘Ahiam son of Sharar the Hararite’ — the variant between Sacar and Sharar is a typical copyist confusion of kāp̄ and rēš. These variants are numerous throughout the parallel lists and generally have no theological significance.
11.36: Hepher the Mecherathite (unattested elsewhere) and Ahijah the Pelonite. The Pelonite appears again at v. 27 (Helez). The frequent ‘-ite’ demonymic forms indicate these warriors are identified by their home towns or clans, not by patronymics — suggesting some are from small or obscure communities.
11.37: Hezro the Carmelite (from Carmel in Judah, 1 Sam 25:2, Nabal’s home) and Naarai son of Ezbai. The Carmelite connection links to Abigail’s homeland. These warriors are from the Judean heartland.
11.38: Joel brother of Nathan — if Nathan here is David’s prophet, this would link a warrior directly to a royal courtier. More likely a different Nathan. Mibhar son of Hagri (2 Sam 23:36 reads differently — possibly scribal). The Hagrites were a trans-Jordanian people (1 Chr 5:10); a Hagrite’s son among David’s elite shows the ethnically inclusive character of the warrior band.
11.39: Zelek the Ammonite — an Ammonite among David’s elite warriors. Naharai the Berothite, armor-bearer of Joab son of Zeruiah. The presence of an Ammonite (enemy nation) and a servant of Joab in the Thirty reflects the diverse and personally loyal character of David’s military family.
11.40: Ira the Ithrite and Gareb the Ithrite — two warriors from the same clan appearing together. The Ithrites were a Judahite sub-group (2 Sam 23:38).
11.41: ʾûriyyāh haḥittî — Uriah the Hittite appears at the very end of the elite warrior roster. In 2 Sam 23:39, he is the thirty-seventh and last named. This placement is almost certainly intentional in the literary ordering — Uriah the Hittite, the most loyal of all the Mighty Men, the one David had murdered. The Chronicler reproduces the list including Uriah; he omits the Bathsheba narrative (as he omits David’s failures generally) but includes Uriah’s name in the honor roll.
11.42: Adina son of Shiza the Reubenite, chief of the Reubenites, and thirty with him. The Reubenite contingent has its own sub-commander. This entry marks the transition to a broader military survey that extends beyond the original core group.
11.43: Hanan son of Maacah and Joshaphat the Mithnite. Maacah is a place-name (Aram-maacah, north of Dan) as well as a personal name. These names appear only here.
11.44: Uzzia the Ashterathite (from Ashtaroth in Bashan, trans-Jordan — Manassite territory), Shama and Jeiel sons of Hothan the Aroerite (Aroer, different from Gad’s Aroer — possibly the Judean Aroer of 1 Sam 30:28).
11.45: Jediael son of Shimri and Joha his brother the Tizite. ‘Tizite’ is unattested elsewhere; possibly a corrupt form of another place-name.
11.46: Eliel the Mahavite; sons of Elnaam: Jeribai and Joshaviah; Ithmah the Moabite. Another Moabite (cf. Zelek the Ammonite, v. 39) in David’s corps — showing the expanding ethnic scope of the Davidic coalition. The Mahavite clan is otherwise unknown.
11.47: Eliel, Obed, and Jaasiel the Mesobaite conclude the list. ‘Mesobaite’ is unattested; possibly a place-name. The list of Mighty Men (vv. 26-47) totals approximately thirty-seven names, though some are grouped. The Chronicler uses this list to show all-Israel participation in David’s kingdom from the start — consistent with his broader theological agenda.

12.1: wᵉʾēlleh habbāʾîm ʾel-dāwid bᵉṣiqlag — ‘and these are those who came to David at Ziklag.’ Ziklag was the Philistine city given to David during his exile (1 Sam 27:6). bᵉʿôḏ ʿāṣûr mippᵉnê šāʾûl ben-qîš — ‘while still restrained from before Saul son of Kish’ — the fugitive period. The Chronicler includes this section to show that David’s all-Israel coalition began forming before he was king, predating any political opportunity.
12.2: middōšᵉqê qešet — ‘armed with the bow, right-handed and left-handed in hurling stones.’ The Benjaminite warriors’ ambidexterity is a military specialty noted also in Judg 20:16 (the men of Gibeah who could sling a stone to a hair’s breadth). Their joining David from Saul’s own tribe is particularly significant — Benjamin loyalty shifting from Saul to David.
12.3: The list of Benjaminite warriors begins with Ahiezer and Joash sons of Shemaah the Gibeathite. Gibeah, again, is Saul’s city. These Benjaminites from Saul’s hometown came to David, the ultimate statement of cross-tribal loyalty. Jeziel and Pelet sons of Azmaveth — Azmaveth also appears in the Mighty Men list (11:33).
12.4: Ishmaiah the Gibeonite is noted as ‘mighty among the Thirty and over the Thirty’ — i.e., ranking within the elite corps already established. Jeremiah, Jahaziel, Johanan, and Josabad the Gederathite fill out the contingent. The overlapping names with the Mighty Men list suggest the two lists derive from the same military administrative source.
12.5: Eluzai, Jerimoth, Bealiah, Shemariah, and Shephatiah the Haruphite. The Haruph clan is mentioned in Nehemiah 7:24 as returnees from exile — linking these pre-exile warriors to the post-exile community the Chronicler is addressing.
12.6: Elkanah, Isshiah, Azarel, Joezer, and Jashobeam the Korahites. The Korahites were a Levitical guild associated with temple music and gatekeeping (1 Chr 6:22; 9:19). Their appearance in a warrior list suggests the boundary between cultic and military roles was permeable in the early monarchy.
12.7: Joelah and Zebadiah sons of Jeroham from Gedor. Gedor is a Judahite town (4:4) — a further note of Judahite support for David supplementing the Benjaminite contingent.
12.8: ûmin-haggāḏî niphrᵉḏû ʾel-dāwid lamlᵉḏbārāh — ‘from the Gadites there separated themselves to David in the wilderness.’ The desert/wilderness period references David’s time in the Judean wilderness (1 Sam 22-26). These Gadites, from trans-Jordan, crossed to join the fugitive king. gibborê ḥayil anšê ṣāḇāʾ lamilḥāmāh maʿarᵉḵê ṣinnāh warōmaḥ — ‘valiant men, warriors for war, trained to handle shield and spear.’ ûp̄ᵉnêhem pᵉnê ʾaryēh — ‘their faces were like the faces of lions’ — the lion-face simile for fierce warriors.
12.9: The Gadite commanders are listed in order: Ezer the first, Obadiah the second, Eliab the third. The ordinal numbering (hārîʾšônāh, haššᵉnî, haššᵉlîšî, etc.) continues through v. 13, imposing a clear hierarchical structure. This military ranking system shows sophisticated administrative organization during David’s fugitive period.
12.10: Mishmannah the fourth, Jeremiah the fifth — the list continues. The Gadite names recur in the trans-Jordanian genealogies of ch. 5. The Chronicler links these warriors back to their tribal genealogies, showing the all-Israel framework of the David narrative.
12.11: Attai the sixth, Eliel the seventh. The name Eliel appears multiple times in these lists (11:46-47; 12:11) — likely different individuals sharing a common theophoric name (‘my God is God’).
12.12: Johanan the eighth, Elzabad the ninth. The list of Gadite officers reaches its tenth at v. 13. The eleven-man named unit reflects a military decimal structure (cf. the tribal warrior counts in chs. 7 and 12:23-37).
12.13: Jeremiah the tenth, Machbannai the eleventh. The complete Gadite officer list has eleven men. Verse 14’s summary notes they ranged from commanders of a hundred to commanders of a thousand, showing these were mid-level officers, not common soldiers.
12.14: min-bᵉnê-gāḏ rāʾšê haṣṣāḇāʾ — ‘from the sons of Gad, commanders of the army.’ The superlative: haqqāṭōn lᵉmeʾāh wᵉhaggāḏôl laʾelep̄ — ‘the least over a hundred and the greatest over a thousand.’ This telescoping of magnitude is a rhetorical device emphasizing the quality of the entire contingent.
12.15: hēmmāh ʿāḇrû ʾet-hayyardēn baḥōḏeš hārîʾšôn — ‘they crossed the Jordan in the first month’ — during spring flooding (kî mālaʾ ʿal kol-gᵉḏôtāyw — ‘when it had overflowed all its banks’). Crossing a flood-stage Jordan to join David was an act of extraordinary commitment. The crossing motif echoes the Exodus/conquest Jordan-crossings as a theological parallel.
12.16: wayyāḇōʾû min-bᵉnê binyāmîn wîhûḏāh — ‘and there came from the sons of Benjamin and Judah to the stronghold.’ The double reference — Benjamin (Saul’s tribe) and Judah (David’s tribe) coming together — signals the beginning of the reunification that will culminate at Hebron.
12.17: David’s interrogation of the arriving men: ʾim-lᵉšālôm bāʾtem ʾēlay lᵉʿāzᵉrēnî — ‘if you have come to me in peace to help me.’ But if they have come to betray him to his enemies: yōkaḥ ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇôtênû — ‘the God of our fathers will see and rebuke.’ David commends the judgment of betrayal to God rather than taking pre-emptive violence — showing the theological restraint that characterizes his fugitive period.
12.18: wᵉrûaḥ lāḇᵉšāh ʾet-ʿămaśay — ‘the Spirit clothed Amasai.’ The verb lāḇaš (to clothe) with Spirit-clothing is the same idiom used for Gideon (Judg 6:34) and Zechariah (2 Chr 24:20). The Spirit ‘wearing’ a person rather than ‘entering’ or ‘coming upon’ them is distinctive. Amasai then speaks a poetic declaration: lᵉḵā dāwid wᵉʿimmᵉḵā ben-yišay šālôm šālôm lᵉḵā — ‘Yours, David, and with you, son of Jesse! Peace, peace to you.’ The three-fold šālôm is a covenantal blessing formula.
12.19: Some Manassites defect to David as he goes with the Philistines to fight Saul (the battle of Jezreel/Gilboa context, 1 Sam 29). The Philistine lords dismissed David out of concern he might defect. bᵉḇôʾô ʿim-pᵉlištîm — ‘when he came with the Philistines’ — the Chronicler does not explain the awkward context (David as Philistine vassal) but includes the result: Manassites joined him.
12.20: The Manassite defectors named: Adnah, Jozabad, Jediael, Michael, Jozabad (second), Elihu, Zillethai — ‘heads of thousands’ (rāʾšê hāʾălāpîm) in Manasseh. These are senior military commanders changing allegiance from Saul to David, a major political shift.
12.21: wᵉhēmmāh ʿāzᵉrû ʾet-dāwid ʿal-haggdûḏ — ‘they helped David against the raiding band.’ The reference is to the Amalekite raid on Ziklag (1 Sam 30), where David recovered his people and plunder. kî gibbôrê ḥayil kull̄ām — ‘for they were all mighty warriors.’
